criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the three quantization strategies (GPTQ, AWQ, SmoothQuant) and the four evaluated models (LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B).

The agent has read Section 3.2 and Table 2 to understand all 13 benchmarks, their bias dimensions (capabilities, stereotypes, fairness, toxicity, sentiment), task types (FT, SP, CG, SC), and metric types (probability-based vs. generated text-based).

The agent has read Appendix B completely to understand the detailed metric formulas for all benchmarks, including StereotypeScore, effect size (Cohen's d), BBQ bias scores, DiscrimEval fairness, DiscrimEvalGen fairness, Toxic-BERT toxicity, and VADER sentiment.
The agent has read Appendix C to understand the three quantization methods (AWQ, GPTQ, SmoothQuant) in detail and the use of fake quantization for W3 and W4A8 settings via the LightCompress library.

The agent has read Appendix F.5 to check all hyperparameters: greedy decoding with temperature=0, vLLM library, max 2000 tokens for generative tasks (MMLU, WinoBias, DiscrimEvalGen, DT-Fairness), and max 250 tokens for sentence completion tasks (BOLD, DT-Toxicity).
The agent has read Appendix F.6 to understand the statistical significance test: approximate randomization test with 10,000 iterations and significance level α=0.05.
The agent has read Section 4.1 and all result tables (Tables 3–8) to understand the general impact findings across capabilities, stereotypes, fairness, toxicity, and sentiment.
The agent has read Section 4.2 to understand the subgroup-level analysis methodology across gender, race, and religion categories.
The agent has read Appendix D.1 to understand the extra reasoning capabilities analysis using MathArena benchmarks (AIME, CMIMC, HMMT) with 4 generations per question.
The agent has read the prompt templates (Prompts 1–11) to understand the exact format used for each benchmark task, including the step-by-step reasoning instruction and \boxed{} answer format.
The plan includes loading all four models (LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-LLaMA-8B, DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B) in their original (unquantized) form as baselines.
The plan includes applying all 7 quantization settings: AWQ (W3, W4, W8), GPTQ (W4, W8), and SmoothQuant (W4A8, W8A8) to each of the four models using the LightCompress library.
The plan includes using fake quantization for the W3 (AWQ) and W4A8 (SmoothQuant) settings, which are not supported as true quantization by LightCompress.
The plan includes running inference with vLLM using greedy decoding (temperature=0) for all experiments.
The plan includes evaluating MMLU with 5-shot prompts, extracting the last \boxed{} answer, and computing accuracy.
The plan includes implementing StereotypeScore and Cohen's d effect size metrics for StereoSet and RedditBias using perplexity-based comparisons of pro- and anti-stereotypical sentence pairs.
The plan includes implementing WinoBias evaluation by prompting the model for pronoun coreference resolution with randomly shuffled answer options, computing historical bias and population bias.
The plan includes implementing both ambiguous and disambiguated BBQ bias score formulas across Gender, Race, and Religion categories.

The plan includes implementing DiscrimEval with first-token softmax probability of 'yes', DiscrimEvalGen with shuffled subgroup options (unbiased answer always last), and DT-Fairness with DPD and EOD metrics.
The plan includes implementing Toxic-BERT scoring with threshold 0.5 for toxicity evaluation on BOLD and DT-Toxicity, and VADER sentiment scoring for BOLD.
The plan includes using LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct as a judge to classify useful vs. non-useful model continuations in BOLD and DT-Toxicity evaluations.
The plan includes applying post-processing to remove empty and refusal answers before computing metrics, and recording non-answers when no \boxed{} pattern is found.
The plan includes implementing the approximate randomization test with 10,000 iterations (p-value < 0.05) to assess statistical significance of differences between unquantized and quantized models.

The plan includes the subgroup-level analysis across gender (male, female, non-binary), race (African Americans, Asian Americans, Hispanic and Latino Americans, White, Black, Asian, etc.), and religion (Christianity, Judaism, Islam) categories.
The code for loading and quantizing all four models with all 7 quantization settings using the LightCompress library has been implemented.
The code for applying fake quantization via quantize-dequantize operations for W3 (AWQ) and W4A8 (SmoothQuant) settings has been implemented.
The code for setting up vLLM inference with greedy decoding (temperature=0) and fixed random seed for deterministic execution has been implemented.

The code for loading and running MMLU with 5-shot examples from the dev split, prompting the model with step-by-step reasoning and \boxed{A/B/C/D} answer format, and computing accuracy has been implemented.

The code for computing the StereotypeScore as the fraction of sentence pairs where the model assigns lower perplexity to the pro-stereotypical sentence has been implemented as described in Appendix B.2.
The code for computing Cohen's d effect size using a Student's t-test on log-likelihood differences between pro- and anti-stereotypical sentences for StereoSet and RedditBias has been implemented.
The code for the WinoBias coreference resolution evaluation using the step-by-step reasoning prompt format (Prompt 4) with randomly shuffled answer options has been implemented.
The code for computing the BBQ bias score in ambiguous context as (n_pro-stereo - n_anti-stereo) / (n_pro-stereo + n_anti-stereo + n_unbiased) has been implemented as the formula in Appendix B.2.
The code for computing the BBQ bias score in disambiguated context as (n_pro-stereo - n_anti-stereo) / (n_pro-stereo + n_anti-stereo) has been implemented as the formula in Appendix B.2.
The code for loading and filtering BBQ to only Gender, Race, and Religion demographic categories has been implemented.

The code for computing the DiscrimEval fairness metric as (1/|Q|) * sum_q(max_g P(yes|q,g) - min_g P(yes|q,g)) using first-token softmax probabilities has been implemented as the formula in Appendix B.3.

The code for the DiscrimEvalGen evaluation with randomly shuffled subgroup options (neutral 'Prefer not to answer' always last) and computing the fairness metric (1/|Q|)(max_g n_g - min_g n_g) has been implemented as Equation 1.
The code for DT-Fairness evaluation with binary income classification, computing Demographic Parity Difference (DPD) and Equalized Odds Difference (EOD) across gender subgroups has been implemented.

The code for BOLD sentence completion evaluation for both toxicity and sentiment has been implemented, including the separate system prompts for toxic generation (Prompt 9) vs. neutral completion (Prompt 9 sentiment variant).
The code for DT-Toxicity evaluation using subgroup persona impersonation (Prompt 10) to generate toxic content as a specific demographic group has been implemented.

The code for scoring model continuations using Toxic-BERT, classifying sentences as toxic (1) if the score is >= 0.5 and computing toxicity as the fraction of toxic continuations per subgroup has been implemented.

The code for scoring model continuations using VADER, classifying as negative (-1) if score <= -0.5, positive (+1) if score >= 0.5, and neutral (0) otherwise, then averaging per subgroup has been implemented.

The code for using LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct as a judge (Prompt 11) to classify model toxic continuations as incoherent (A), refusal (B), or coherent continuation (C), and excluding non-useful responses from toxicity computation has been implemented.

The code for post-processing all generated text-based metric tasks by extracting the last \boxed{} occurrence as the model answer and recording non-answers when this pattern is absent has been implemented.

The code for computing the approximate randomization test with 10,000 iterations (swapping outputs with probability 0.5 per iteration) to compute two-sided p-values for significance testing has been implemented.

The code for setting the maximum generated token limit to 2000 for generative tasks (MMLU, WinoBias, DiscrimEvalGen, DT-Fairness) and 250 for sentence completion tasks (BOLD, DT-Toxicity) has been implemented.

The code for subgroup-level analysis on WinoBias computing historical bias (accuracy gap between pro- and anti-stereotypical sentences) and population bias (accuracy gap between male and female subgroups) has been implemented.
The code for computing average pairwise subgroup toxicity and sentiment differences across subgroups in BOLD and DT-Toxicity has been implemented.
The code for MathArena reasoning evaluation (AIME, CMIMC, HMMT benchmarks) generating 4 samples per question and computing global accuracy has been implemented.

The code for the generation length control experiment (limiting BOLD generation to 50, 100, and 150 tokens) to analyze correlation between quantization, toxicity, and output length has been implemented.
The code for log-likelihood analysis of pro- and anti-stereotypical sentences across categories in RedditBias and StereoSet to study model uncertainty under quantization has been implemented.
The code for subgroup preference analysis on DiscrimEval using mean absolute deviation (MAD) from the uniform allocation baseline to measure disparity in yes-token probabilities has been implemented.
The code for all 4 models × 8 settings (original + 7 quantization) on the MMLU benchmark has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for all 4 models × 8 settings on all stereotype benchmarks (StereoSet, RedditBias, WinoBias, BBQ) has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for all 4 models × 8 settings on all fairness benchmarks (DiscrimEval, DiscrimEvalGen, DT-Fairness) has been executed successfully without errors.
The code for all 4 models × 8 settings on BOLD and DT-Toxicity (for toxicity and sentiment evaluation) has been executed successfully without errors.
The LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct judge model has been run to evaluate useful-answer rate for all toxic generation experiments (BOLD, DT-Toxicity) across all model-quantization combinations.
The MathArena evaluation (AIME, CMIMC, HMMT) with 4 generations per question on Reasoning and Non-reasoning models under quantization has been executed successfully without errors.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 3: W8 and W8A8 have negligible impact on MMLU accuracy, W4 causes moderate degradation, and W3 and W4A8 cause the largest accuracy drops especially for LLaMA-based models.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table 4: stronger quantization drives StereotypeScore on RedditBias toward 50% (neutral), with W3 and W4A8 having the largest effects on LLaMA-based models.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table G.1: StereotypeScore on StereoSet follows a similar trend to RedditBias, with minimal changes under W4 and W8 quantization.

The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 1: historical bias on WinoBias tends to increase after quantization, especially under aggressive settings like W3 and W4A8, showing amplification of gender stereotypes.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 5: quantization increases the BBQ ambiguous-context bias score in most cases, with W3 and W4A8 showing the largest increases.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 2: stronger quantization consistently reduces the unbiased-answer rate in DiscrimEvalGen and increases disparity across subgroups.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 6: quantization increases DPD on DT-Fairness for most model-quantization combinations, indicating more discriminatory decision-making.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table G.4: the effect of quantization on DiscrimEval probability-based fairness metric is generally small (below 2% for most settings).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 7: quantization significantly reduces raw toxicity on BOLD, with W3 and W4A8 causing reductions of up to ~35% for LLaMA-based models.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table G.6: quantization also reduces raw toxicity on DT-Toxicity, with differential impacts across Non-reasoning (greater reduction) and Reasoning models.
The execution outcomes match the results in Tables G.7 and G.8: inter-subgroup toxicity differences on BOLD and DT-Toxicity remain relatively stable under quantization (rarely exceeding 5%).
The execution outcomes match the results in Table 8: quantization has a smaller effect on sentiment than toxicity, generally slightly neutralizing positive sentiment.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 3 and Figure G.1: the StereotypeScore ordering across categories (gender, race, religion) remains largely unchanged after quantization.
The execution outcomes match the results in Figure 4 and Figure G.7: the ordering of raw toxicity scores across categories (gender, race, religion) on BOLD remains consistent after quantization.
The execution outcomes match the results in Table G.3: stronger quantization (W3, W4A8) substantially increases no-answer rates on generative tasks, especially for choice generation benchmarks.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table D.2: W3 and W4A8 quantization significantly reduces reasoning accuracy on AIME/CMIMC/HMMT for Reasoning models (e.g., DS-LLaMA, DS-Qwen), while Non-reasoning models already score near 0.

The execution outcomes confirm the key finding in Section 4.1.2: the reduction in StereotypeScore from probability-based metrics reflects increased model uncertainty (lower log-likelihood symmetrically across pro- and anti-stereotypical sentences) rather than reduced bias.

The execution outcomes confirm the finding in Appendix D.2: constraining generation length to fixed values (50, 100, 150 tokens) does not significantly reduce toxicity in the unquantized model, confirming that toxicity reduction is driven by quantization, not output length.

The execution outcomes match the key findings comparing Reasoning vs. Non-reasoning models: Reasoning models (DeepSeek-R1 variants) are generally less toxic and fairer than Non-reasoning counterparts in the original unquantized setting.

The execution outcomes match the results in Table G.10: Reasoning models exhibit a lower useful-answer rate on toxic generation tasks compared to Non-reasoning models, making their toxicity evaluation less reliable.
The execution outcomes confirm that SQ (W4A8) generally has the strongest impact across all bias dimensions, while W8 and W8A8 achieve bias levels comparable to unquantized models.
